description: The controller supports operation as a PCIe Root Complex controlling downstream endpoints. Software selects RC mode via device-level configuration bits before link training. RC mode enables bus enumeration, configuration space setup, and interrupt/message processing per PCIe specifications. The initialization flow details clock/power enable, SerDes setup, and LTSSM enable. Post-training, the RC initiates enumeration and configures downstream ports.
evidence: Section 2.11.1 Operations: PCIe as Root Complex; DEVSTAT.PCIESSMODE; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]
dependencies: Power domain enabled, PCIESSEN set, PLL reference clock present
Remarks: Ensure RC mode is selected prior to LTSSM enable and SerDes PLL lock.

description: The controller can operate as a PCIe Endpoint connected to an upstream Root Complex. In EP mode, it supports legacy INTx and MSI generation towards the RC. Function-level configuration determines the chosen interrupt mechanism during enumeration. EP mode participates in link training and standard PCIe transactions. Software programs capabilities and BARs through configuration space.
evidence: Section 2.14.2 Interrupt Generation in EP Mode; Chapter 2 Operations (EP context implied); Configuration Space chapter refs
dependencies: LTSSM enabled, EP configuration registers programmed
Remarks: Select one interrupt mechanism (INTx or MSI) per function as per PCIe spec.

feature_name: LTSSM control and link training enable
description: Link training is controlled by the LTSSM enable bit in the command/status register. Software disables LTSSM while programming configuration, then enables it to start negotiation. Successful link training is observed via a specific LTSSM state code in a debug register. The mechanism ensures deterministic initialization sequencing. Hardware deasserts LTSSM on reset for safe bring-up.
evidence: Section 2.11.1.1 Initialization Sequence; CMD_STATUS[LTSSM_EN]; DEBUG0[LTSSM_STATE]
dependencies: SerDes PLL locked, proper mode set (RC/EP)
Remarks: Enable LTSSM only after clocks/power and SerDes are stable.

feature_name: LTSSM state visibility via DEBUG registers
description: The controller exposes LTSSM state encoding through debug registers for runtime visibility. Software can poll the DEBUG0 register to detect training progress and completion. Appendix A documents the mapping of codes to states. This aids diagnostics and automated recovery. It is essential for verifying link bring-up.
evidence: DEBUG0[LTSSM_STATE]; Appendix A LTSSM States
dependencies: LTSSM enabled and running
Remarks: Use state codes to implement timeouts and error handling during training.

feature_name: SerDes PLL configuration and lock detection
description: SerDes PLL settings are programmed through dedicated configuration registers. Software enables the PLL and waits for the lock status bit before link training. This ensures stable reference for high-speed lanes. The process is part of the mandatory initialization flow. Misconfiguration prevents link establishment.
evidence: Section 2.3 SerDes Configuration; PCIE_SERDES_CFGPLL; PCIE_SERDES_STS[LOCK]; Section 2.11.1.1 steps 4–5
dependencies: Reference clock provided; power domain enabled
Remarks: Validate reference clock frequency and poll lock before enabling LTSSM.

description: The port supports selectable lane width for link negotiation. A lane enable field allows choosing x1 or x2 configurations. This enables performance scaling and compatibility with partners. Incorrect settings may limit link width. Software must program lanes before training.
evidence: Port Logic Register field LN_EN (1h = x1, 2h = x2); Chapter 3 Port Logic Registers
dependencies: SerDes lanes powered and PLL locked
Remarks: Match lane count with board routing and partner capabilities.

description: After initial Gen1 link-up, software can request a directed speed change to Gen2. A control bit instructs the LTSSM to initiate speed transition. This allows optimizing performance post-stability checks. Partners must support Gen2 for success. Speed change occurs under LTSSM control using training sequences.
evidence: Port Logic Register field DIR_SPD; Section 2.1/2.11 protocol operations; Table showing DIR_SPD description
dependencies: Stable Gen1 link, partner Gen2 capable
Remarks: Gate speed change on signal integrity checks and error rates.

feature_name: PHY TX swing and de-emphasis control
description: Transmit characteristics can be tuned via configuration bits. TX swing selects full or low swing voltage levels. De-emphasis level can be adjusted for upstream ports. Proper tuning improves eye quality and link robustness. Settings should align with board/channel characteristics.
evidence: Port Logic bits CFG_TX_SWING, DEEMPH; Chapter 3 Port Logic Registers
dependencies: SerDes configured, channel characteristics known
Remarks: Use compliance testing to validate settings for target backplane/cable.

feature_name: Number of Fast Training Sequences (FTS) control
description: The controller exposes a field to program the number of fast training sequences. Adjusting FTS can assist interoperability and speed changes. It affects the training duration and partner expectations. Conservative values improve compatibility at cost of time. This is part of port logic configuration.
evidence: Port Logic field NUM_FTS; Chapter 3 Port Logic Registers
dependencies: LTSSM disabled during programming
Remarks: Use vendor-recommended defaults unless interoperability demands tuning.

feature_name: Clock control and module power domain gating
description: The PCIe module requires its power and clock domains to be enabled before use. A device-level enable bit allows software to turn on the PCIe subsystem. Clock control ensures SerDes gets a valid reference before PLL lock. Proper sequencing prevents undefined behavior. Device-specific PSC controls are referenced for full details.
evidence: Section 2.2 Clock Control; Section 2.11.1 steps 1–2; DEVSTAT.PCIESSEN; device PSC guide
dependencies: System PSC configuration available; ref clock present
Remarks: Follow device PSC/user guide to enable domains prior to config.

description: The document prescribes reset handling for reliable bring-up. Software must keep LTSSM disabled on reset and reinitialize configuration registers. Link retraining and subsystem reset are recommended after link-down events. Proper reset isolates stale application state. This ensures clean recovery paths.
evidence: Section 2.13 Reset Considerations; Section 2.14.6.1 Link Down Interrupt guidance
dependencies: Clock/power domains stable after reset
Remarks: Implement full software re-init on link-down as advised.

description: The controller supports PCIe power management with event notification. Software is alerted to power transitions via a dedicated interrupt. Proper handling maintains link integrity and latency requirements. Capabilities are configured in configuration space per PCIe spec. Entry/exit sequencing follows standard PME flows.
evidence: Section 2.15 Power Management; Section 2.14.6.3 Power Management Event Interrupt
dependencies: PM capabilities enabled in config space
Remarks: Coordinate PM with partners to avoid performance surprises.

feature_name: Address translation between local and PCIe spaces
description: The architecture provides programmable address translation mechanisms. These map system addresses to PCIe address spaces and vice versa. Translation is essential for DMA and host access to internal memories. Configuration must prevent overlap and ensure protection boundaries. The document outlines translation tables and usage guidance.
evidence: Section 2.7 Address Translation; Section 2.8 PCIe Address Spaces
dependencies: Correct base/limit programming; coherent with system MMU
Remarks: Define non-overlapping inbound/outbound windows aligned to BAR usage.

feature_name: PCIe address spaces and configuration space layout
description: The module implements standard PCIe address spaces including configuration. Register access offsets remain consistent regardless of access mode. A 1 KB configuration space per function is provided per PCIe spec context. This enables standard enumeration and capability discovery. Software relies on these layouts for portability.
evidence: Section 2.8 PCIe Address Spaces (note on 1 KB configuration space offsets); page 2-19 reference
dependencies: Bus enumeration active; RC access available
Remarks: Use standard config cycles to probe capabilities and BARs.

description: The controller supports standard PCIe request and completion transactions. Memory reads and writes are issued/received according to the protocol. Ordering, tags, and completions follow PCIe rules. This forms the basis for DMA and CPU-initiated transfers. The section outlines transaction flows and constraints.
evidence: Section 2.10 PCIe Transactions
dependencies: Link trained; address translation configured
Remarks: Observe ordering and completion timeout rules per spec.

feature_name: MSI interrupt generation in Endpoint mode
description: In Endpoint mode, MSI interrupts are generated via 32-bit memory writes to programmed MSI address/data. System software sets up MSI capability during enumeration. The EP then signals events by issuing the MSI write. Only one interrupt mechanism (MSI or legacy) is active per function. Registers provide enable and status control.
evidence: Section 2.14.2.2 MSI Interrupt Generation in EP Mode; MSI IRQ enable/status registers (Chapter 3, e.g., Fig 3-42/3-43)
dependencies: MSI capability enabled and configured by RC
Remarks: Ensure MSI address/data are valid before issuing MSI writes.

feature_name: Legacy INTx interrupt generation in Endpoint mode
description: Endpoints can assert legacy INTx via in-band messages. Software enables legacy generation and toggles set/clear registers to assert/deassert. Only one INTx can be pending until deassert completes. The RC interprets messages as INTA/B/C/D based on configuration. Delivery latency is message-based and not electrically guaranteed.
evidence: Section 2.14.2.1 Legacy Interrupt Generation in EP Mode; LEGACY_X_IRQ_ENABLE_SET; EP_IRQ_SET/CLR/STATUS
dependencies: Legacy interrupt mode selected during enumeration
Remarks: Do not reassert before deassert; handle pending status correctly.

feature_name: Advanced Error Reporting (AER) interrupt in RC
description: When enabled, the RC receives AER interrupts for reported errors. Software must set the Root Error Command register to accept AER messages. The interrupt can arrive via MSI or internal INTx. Proper handling categorizes severity and initiates recovery. Enabling is optional but recommended for robustness.
evidence: Section 2.14.5.3 Advanced Error Reporting Interrupt; RC_ERR_CMD; Section 2.16 Error Handling
dependencies: AER enabled in capabilities; RC interrupt route configured
Remarks: Log, classify, and act per PCIe AER guidelines.

feature_name: Link down detection and recovery interrupt
description: A dedicated interrupt notifies software of PHY link loss. Recommended action is to reset and reinitialize the PCIe subsystem. Application state must be reestablished before resuming transfers. This ensures clean renegotiation and stability. The guidance standardizes recovery flow.
evidence: Section 2.14.6.1 Link down Interrupt
dependencies: Interrupt controller wired; reset path available
Remarks: Trigger full reinit and retraining on link-down events.

description: Timeouts or aborts on PCIe transactions generate an interrupt. Software handles these based on error severity and context. Proper classification avoids data corruption. Recovery may involve retries or link resets. The PCIe specification defines detailed handling rules.
evidence: Section 2.14.6.2 Transaction Error Interrupts; Section 2.16 Error Handling
dependencies: Interrupt enabled; error logging registers accessible
Remarks: Implement retry limits and escalate to reset on persistent faults.

description: Power management transitions generate an interrupt to system software. Handlers coordinate device state changes with the link partner. Correct sequencing minimizes latency and preserves data integrity. Capabilities and PME configuration come from config space. This integrates with broader system PM policies.
evidence: Section 2.14.6.3 Power Management Event Interrupt; Section 2.15
dependencies: PME enable set; interrupt routing configured
Remarks: Throttle or pause DMA during low-power entries to avoid faults.

description: The module supports DMA data movement using PCIe read/write transactions. System DMA engines can source or sink TLPs through mapped address windows. The guide discusses DMA considerations for throughput and ordering. Proper translation and BAR mapping are prerequisites. This enables high-bandwidth transfers between peers.
evidence: Section 2.9 DMA Support
dependencies: Address translation configured; BARs/windows mapped
Remarks: Use large, aligned bursts and doorbells to amortize overheads.

description: A loopback feature allows routing traffic for self-test and validation. This aids board bring-up and PHY integrity checks. Software enables loopback through control registers. It operates without external partners in certain modes. Results inform tuning and diagnostics.
evidence: Section 2.12 PCIe Loopback
dependencies: SerDes configured; proper test sequencing
Remarks: Disable loopback before normal operation to avoid routing anomalies.

description: The document provides guidance for emulation environments. It covers special cases for visibility and safe stepping. Debug registers expose internal states helpful during bring-up. These facilities complement standard PCIe diagnostics. Proper use accelerates issue isolation.
evidence: Section 2.17 Emulation Considerations; DEBUG registers (e.g., DEBUG0)
dependencies: Emulation tools connected; debug access available
Remarks: Integrate debug polling into bring-up scripts for automation.

description: A defined sequence brings the RC online from reset. It includes enabling power/clock, setting mode bits, programming SerDes, and enabling LTSSM. Software then verifies link-up via state registers. Enumeration follows to configure downstream devices. Only after these steps should DMA begin.
evidence: Section 2.11.1.1 Initialization Sequence steps 1–12
dependencies: PSC enabled; SerDes locked; LTSSM enabled
Remarks: Adhere strictly to step order to avoid intermittent failures.

description: The controller’s pins may be multiplexed and require proper selection. Software or boot configuration selects PCIe functions on shared pins. Correct muxing is prerequisite for signal integrity and link-up. The document points to device-specific details. This step often precedes SerDes activation.
evidence: Section 2.6 Pin Multiplexing; device data manual references
dependencies: Board design supports PCIe pins; mux set before enable
Remarks: Confirm no contention with other peripherals on shared pins.

description: Signal roles and directions are enumerated to aid hardware integration. These include lane pairs, reference clocks, resets, and sideband signals. Understanding them ensures correct board routing and termination. The section complements SerDes configuration guidance. Compliance depends on adhering to these details.
evidence: Section 2.5 Signal Descriptions
dependencies: Board design matches signal requirements
Remarks: Follow signal polarity and reset timing per descriptions.

description: The module implements standard PCIe configuration headers and capabilities. Software accesses these to enable features like MSI and AER. Register offsets are consistent across access modes. This supports OS and firmware enumeration flows. Capability structures drive feature negotiation.
evidence: Chapter 3 Registers; note on consistent offsets and 1 KB config space (page 2-19)
dependencies: RC access path; function powered
Remarks: Do not assume device-specific offsets; use capability traversal.

description: The controller supports error detection and reporting per PCIe spec. It categorizes errors and can escalate via interrupts. Software should consult error status and take corrective action. Integration with AER enhances visibility. Proper handling maintains data integrity and uptime.
evidence: Section 2.16 Error Handling; Section 2.14.5.3 for AER linkage
dependencies: Error status registers accessible; interrupts enabled
Remarks: Record context and counters to support post-mortem analysis.}
